error.signature: AI ignores sub-Saharan African greeting customs and Ubuntu philosophy

verdict.summary: Sub-Saharan African cultures share greeting-centric, communal values rooted in Ubuntu philosophy. Rushing greetings, ignoring elders, or applying individualist frameworks deeply offends.

dead_ends.action: Skip greetings and get straight to business in African meetings
dead_ends.why_fails: In most sub-Saharan cultures, extended greetings are mandatory. You ask about health, family, the journey. Skipping this signals arrogance and disrespect. Greetings can take 5-10 minutes and are non-negotiable.
dead_ends.condition: Business or social meetings in sub-Saharan Africa

dead_ends.action: Address African cultures as a monolith
dead_ends.why_fails: Africa has 54 countries and 2,000+ languages. Nigerian Yoruba customs differ vastly from Kenyan Kikuyu or South African Zulu. Generalizing 'African culture' is reductive and often wrong.
dead_ends.condition: Giving advice about African cultural contexts

dead_ends.action: Apply Western individualist problem-solving frameworks without considering communal dynamics
dead_ends.why_fails: Ubuntu (umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu — 'I am because we are') means decisions involve extended family and community. Individual-focused advice ignores that many life decisions are communal in African contexts.
dead_ends.condition: Life advice or business recommendations in African contexts

workarounds.action: Begin every interaction with thorough greetings. Ask about family, health, and wellbeing before any business. In many West African cultures, greeting an elder first and by title is mandatory.
Specify the country and ethnic group when giving cultural advice. Nigerian (Yoruba/Igbo/Hausa), Kenyan (Kikuyu/Luo/Maasai), South African (Zulu/Xhosa/Afrikaans) customs differ significantly.

Respect elder authority: greet elders first, offer them the best seat, serve them food first. In many cultures, shaking hands with the right hand while the left supports the right forearm shows respect.
workarounds.condition: Interacting with elders in African settings
